summary: Joel Spolsky provides a real-time personal account from Manhattan on September 11, 2001. Writing from the Upper West Side, about 5 miles from Ground Zero, he describes the surreal quiet of the city, streams of people walking home in business clothes, and the complete shutdown of mass transit. He recounts friends scattered across the city trying to get home, and ends with a sobering conversation with a policeman about the hundreds of first responders killed in the collapse.
key_points: Blood donation centers were immediately overwhelmed, turning away volunteers
The city's transportation infrastructure completely shut down, stranding thousands and forcing people to walk miles to get home
Despite the horror downtown, life continued in strange normalcy uptown — going to dinner, trying to get home
A policeman reported 400 police and firefighters killed while rescuing people inside the buildings, and shared the haunting memory of hearing trapped officers' screams over the radio
money_quotes: Up here on the Upper West Side, about 5 miles north of Ground Zero, I saw streams of people in business clothes walking home.
I saw people planning to walk all the way up to the George Washington bridge and then across to New Jersey where they lived.
The streets were very quiet around here, there was very little traffic.
He said he will always remember the screams he heard from trapped police over the radio.
He said that 400 policemen and firemen were killed when the buildings collapsed. They were in there rescuing people.
key_insight: A firsthand account of September 11th from a New Yorker capturing the eerie calm, logistical chaos, and human toll as the city tried to process an incomprehensible event in real time.
